quote: Instead of my quiet office, I'd go out on the train and do it in public, with people staring at me.
tension: He could already memorize a shuffled deck in seconds. So why make practice harder?
situation: Nelson kept winning. Then he started losing — and not because his memory failed. *There've been competitions where I won, and ones where I lost based on the stress.* The skill was there. The stress was taking it away.
principle: He didn't need a better memory. He needed to reach the memory he already had — while his heart pounded and strangers stared. So he stopped practicing in calm, and started *practicing the pressure*.
brain: Pressure doesn't erase a skill. It *blocks your access* to it. Under stress, the brain's self-monitoring gets loud and grabs the very attention the skill needs. Train only in calm, and calm is the only place the skill shows up.
reframe: This is why practicing alone in your room will never make you ready for the stage. The room has none of the things that make the stage hard. *You have to practice the pressure, not just the part.*
ctaPunch: The skill isn't the problem. *Access* is.
